intent: Check whether the candidate can define a Node.js module as a reusable code boundary with imports and exports, not just a file on disk.
primaryQuestion: What is a module in Node.js?

expectedSignals.label: Defines a module as a unit of JavaScript code that can expose values such as functions, classes, constants, or objects for other files to use.

requirements.description: Defines a module as a separate unit or file of JavaScript code
Mentions exposing functions, classes, constants, objects, or configuration for other files
Connects modules to reuse across files instead of copying code

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that Node.js has built-in modules such as fs and path, third-party modules installed from npm, and local modules created in the project.

requirements.description: Names a built-in Node.js module such as fs, path, http, or crypto
Names a third-party npm package used by a project
Names a local module the team might create, such as a service, utility, route, or config helper

expectedSignals.label: Explains that modules help organize code, avoid putting everything in one file, and create clearer boundaries between responsibilities.

requirements.description: Explains that modules organize code by responsibility
Explains why putting all logic in one file becomes hard to maintain
Mentions clear boundaries between API, service, utility, config, or data access responsibilities

expectedSignals.label: Connects modules to importing and exporting through CommonJS require/module.exports or ES module import/export.

requirements.description: Mentions CommonJS require and module.exports
Mentions ES module import and export syntax
Explains that one file exports values and another imports them for use

expectedSignals.label: Understands that a module has its own scope, so top-level variables are not automatically global.

requirements.description: States that each Node.js module has its own scope
Explains that top-level variables in a module are not automatically global
Explains that values must be explicitly exported to be consumed elsewhere

scoringHints.description: Clearly explains module scope, reuse, built-in versus third-party versus local modules, and how exports/imports connect files.
Gives a correct reusable-code definition but misses module scope or the different module sources.
Can say modules organize code but cannot explain how one module exposes code to another.
Answer shows no practical understanding of Node.js module boundaries.

followUps.question: Can you name one built-in module, one npm module, and one local module you might create in an API project?
followUps.purpose: Force the candidate to distinguish module sources concretely.

followUps.question: If you declare a variable at the top of a module file, is it automatically global in Node.js?

en.title: Node.js module basics
en.displayQuestion: What is a module in Node.js?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands modules as scoped, reusable code units.
guidance: Define a module as a code unit with explicit imports and exports.
Mention built-in, npm, and local modules.
Explain module scope and code organization benefits.
commonMistakes: Saying modules are only npm packages.
Assuming top-level variables are global.
Not explaining exports and imports.

vi.title: Nền tảng module trong Node.js
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có hiểu module là đơn vị code có scope riêng và có thể tái sử dụng hay không.
guidance: Định nghĩa module là đơn vị code có import và export rõ ràng.
Nhắc tới module built-in, module npm và module local.
Giải thích scope riêng của module và lợi ích tổ chức code.
commonMistakes: Nói module chỉ là npm package.
Tưởng biến top-level tự động là global.
Không giải thích được export và import.

ja.title: Node.js module の基礎
ja.displayQuestion: Node.js における module とは何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: module を scope を持つ再利用可能な code unit として理解しているか確認する。
guidance: module を明示的な import/export を持つ code unit として定義する。
built-in module、npm module、local module に触れる。
module scope と code organization の利点を説明する。
commonMistakes: module は npm package だけだと言う。
top-level 変数が自動的に global になると思う。
export と import を説明できない。

sourceReferences.label: Node.js API: Modules - CommonJS
sourceReferences.note: Used for CommonJS module scope, require, and module.exports behavior.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js API: ECMAScript modules
sourceReferences.note: Used for ES module support in Node.js.

intent: Check whether the candidate can compare CommonJS and ES Modules in Node.js at a practical junior level.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between CommonJS and ES Modules?

expectedSignals.label: Identifies CommonJS as the older Node.js module system using require and module.exports.

requirements.description: Identifies CommonJS as a Node.js module system
Mentions require for importing CommonJS modules
Mentions module.exports for exposing CommonJS values

expectedSignals.label: Identifies ES Modules as the standardized JavaScript module system using import and export.

requirements.description: Identifies ES Modules as the standardized JavaScript module system
Mentions import syntax
Mentions named or default export syntax

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that Node.js decides module mode using file extensions such as .cjs and .mjs or the package.json type field.

requirements.description: Mentions package.json type field and what it changes for .js files
Mentions .cjs and .mjs file extensions
Connects module mode to which import/export style Node will parse

expectedSignals.label: Explains that CommonJS loading is commonly synchronous, while ES Modules are statically analyzable and support features such as top-level await.

requirements.description: Mentions CommonJS require is commonly synchronous
Mentions ES modules are statically analyzable
Mentions ES module support for features such as top-level await

expectedSignals.label: Mentions practical interop issues, such as ESM-only packages not always being require-able from CommonJS and default or named import differences.

requirements.description: Explains what can happen when a CommonJS app tries to require an ESM-only package
Mentions default versus named import/export shape differences
Recommends choosing module mode intentionally instead of mixing styles casually

scoringHints.description: Contrasts syntax, runtime mode selection, loading semantics, and interop risks in clear practical terms.
Knows the two systems and basic syntax but misses module mode detection or interop detail.
Recognizes the names but treats them as interchangeable snippets.
Answer would lead to broken imports in a real Node.js project.

followUps.question: What does the package.json type field change for .js files?
What might happen if a CommonJS app tries to require an ESM-only npm package?
followUps.purpose: Check whether the candidate understands real interoperability failures.

en.title: CommonJS versus ES Modules
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between CommonJS and ES Modules?
en.displayIntent: Check practical understanding of the two Node.js module systems.
guidance: Name the syntax used by each system.
Explain how Node decides module mode.
Mention at least one practical interop issue.
commonMistakes: Reducing the difference to syntax only.
Ignoring package.json type.
Assuming every import direction works automatically.

vi.title: CommonJS và ES Modules
vi.displayQuestion: CommonJS và ES Modules khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết thực tế về hai hệ module trong Node.js.
guidance: Nêu cú pháp của từng hệ module.
Giải thích Node chọn module mode như thế nào.
Nhắc ít nhất một vấn đề interop thực tế.
commonMistakes: Chỉ xem khác biệt là cú pháp.
Bỏ qua package.json type.
Tưởng mọi chiều import đều tự hoạt động.

ja.title: CommonJS と ES Modules
ja.displayQuestion: CommonJS と ES Modules はどう違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: Node.js の 2 つの module system を実務的に理解しているか確認する。
guidance: それぞれの syntax を挙げる。
Node が module mode をどう決めるか説明する。
実際の interop 問題を少なくとも 1 つ述べる。
commonMistakes: 違いは syntax だけだと考える。
package.json type を無視する。
どの方向の import も自動的に動くと思う。

sourceReferences.label: Node.js API: Modules - CommonJS
sourceReferences.note: Used for CommonJS behavior and require semantics.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js API: ECMAScript modules
sourceReferences.note: Used for ES module syntax, loading, and interoperability context.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js API: Packages
sourceReferences.note: Used for package.json type and package boundary behavior.

intent: Verify that the candidate understands the concrete APIs used to consume and expose code in CommonJS and ES Modules.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between require/module.exports and import/export?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that require loads a module in CommonJS and returns the value assigned to module.exports.

requirements.description: Explains require as the CommonJS loading function
Explains that require returns the value assigned to module.exports
Can show a small CommonJS export/import example such as an add function

expectedSignals.label: Explains that module.exports is the value a CommonJS module exposes, while exports is only a shortcut when used carefully.

requirements.description: Explains that module.exports is the actual exported value
Explains that exports is only a shortcut reference to module.exports when used carefully
Explains why assigning exports = myFunction breaks the shortcut instead of exporting the function

expectedSignals.label: Explains that import/export are ES Module declarations for named exports, default exports, and importing bindings from another module.

requirements.description: Mentions named exports and imports
Mentions default exports and imports
Can show a small ES module export/import example

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that import declarations are normally static and placed at the top level, while dynamic import() exists for runtime loading.

requirements.description: Mentions import declarations are normally static and top-level
Mentions dynamic import() for runtime loading
Distinguishes static import declarations from runtime dynamic imports

expectedSignals.label: Understands that projects should avoid mixing styles casually because transpilation and CommonJS/ESM interop can change the imported shape.

requirements.description: Warns against mixing CommonJS and ESM casually
Mentions transpilation or interop can change the imported shape
Recommends following the project's configured module system consistently

scoringHints.description: Explains require return values, module.exports, exports shortcut caveat, ES named/default exports, and dynamic import at a practical level.
Can use both styles correctly but misses one nuance such as exports reassignment or dynamic import.
Has copied syntax before but cannot explain what value is being exported or imported.
Answer would likely produce incorrect import shapes in real code.

followUps.question: Can you write a tiny example that exports an add function and imports it in another file using either module system?
followUps.purpose: Force concrete syntax and exported-shape reasoning.

followUps.question: What is the problem with assigning exports = myFunction in CommonJS?
followUps.purpose: Check understanding of the exports shortcut.

en.title: require/module.exports and import/export
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between require/module.exports and import/export?
en.displayIntent: Check concrete module import and export mechanics.
guidance: Explain CommonJS require and module.exports.
Explain ES Module named and default exports.
Mention exported shape and interop pitfalls.
commonMistakes: Reassigning exports incorrectly.
Confusing default and named exports.
Using import as if it were exactly the same as require.

vi.title: require/module.exports và import/export
vi.displayQuestion: require/module.exports khác import/export như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra cơ chế import và export module ở mức thực tế.
guidance: Giải thích require và module.exports trong CommonJS.
Giải thích named export và default export trong ES Module.
Nhắc tới exported shape và lỗi interop thường gặp.
commonMistakes: Gán lại exports sai cách.
Nhầm default export với named export.
Dùng import như thể giống hệt require.

ja.title: require/module.exports と import/export
ja.displayQuestion: require/module.exports と import/export はどう違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: module の import/export mechanics を具体的に理解しているか確認する。
guidance: CommonJS の require と module.exports を説明する。
ES Module の named export と default export を説明する。
exported shape と interop の落とし穴に触れる。
commonMistakes: exports を誤って再代入する。
default export と named export を混同する。
import を require と完全に同じものとして使う。

sourceReferences.label: Node.js API: Modules - CommonJS
sourceReferences.note: Used for require, module.exports, and exports shortcut behavior.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js API: ECMAScript modules
sourceReferences.note: Used for import/export and ES module semantics.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands package.json as the project manifest used by npm and Node.js tooling.

expectedSignals.label: Describes package.json as the project manifest for a Node.js package or application.

requirements.description: Identifies package.json as the manifest file for a Node.js project or package
Connects the file to project or package identity rather than installed source code
Mentions that npm or Node.js tooling reads this manifest

expectedSignals.label: Mentions dependency declarations such as dependencies and devDependencies.

requirements.description: Mentions the dependencies field for runtime packages
Mentions the devDependencies field for development-time packages
Explains that package declarations tell npm what to install

expectedSignals.label: Mentions scripts such as start, test, build, and dev that standardize common commands.

requirements.description: Mentions the scripts field in package.json
Names common scripts such as start, test, build, dev, or lint
Explains that scripts standardize common project commands for the team

expectedSignals.label: Mentions package metadata such as name, version, license, main, type, engines, or exports depending on the project.

requirements.description: Mentions metadata such as package name, version, or license
Mentions entry or module-related fields such as main, exports, or type
Mentions fields such as engines when discussing runtime compatibility

expectedSignals.label: Explains that npm and other tooling read package.json to install packages, run scripts, and understand package entry points.

requirements.description: Explains that npm reads package.json to install dependencies
Explains that npm reads package.json scripts to run project commands
Explains that Node.js or bundlers use manifest fields to understand package entry points or module mode

scoringHints.description: Explains manifest purpose, dependencies, scripts, metadata, entry points, and tooling integration.
Knows package.json contains dependencies and scripts but misses package metadata or Node entry point fields.
Has seen package.json but cannot explain how npm and Node tooling use it.
Answer misunderstands the file's role or suggests unsafe usage.

followUps.question: Which fields in package.json would you expect to check first when onboarding to a Node.js service?
followUps.purpose: Probe practical familiarity with project manifests.

followUps.question: What can the package.json type field change in a Node.js project?
followUps.purpose: Connect package metadata to module behavior.

en.title: package.json purpose
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands package.json as a Node.js project manifest.
guidance: Mention dependencies and scripts.
Mention metadata and entry point fields.
Explain how npm and tooling use the file.
commonMistakes: Thinking it stores dependency source code.
Thinking it is only for published libraries.
Putting secrets in package.json.

vi.title: Mục đích của package.json
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có hiểu package.json là manifest của project Node.js hay không.
guidance: Nhắc tới dependencies và scripts.
Nhắc tới metadata và entry point.
Giải thích npm và tooling dùng file này như thế nào.
commonMistakes: Tưởng file này chứa source code của dependency.
Tưởng chỉ library publish lên npm mới cần file này.
Đưa secret vào package.json.

ja.title: package.json の目的
ja.displayQuestion: package.json は何に使われますか。
ja.displayIntent: package.json を Node.js project manifest として理解しているか確認する。
guidance: dependencies と scripts に触れる。
metadata と entry point fields に触れる。
npm と tooling がこの file をどう使うか説明する。
commonMistakes: dependency source code を保存する file だと思う。
公開 library だけに必要だと思う。
package.json に secret を入れる。

sourceReferences.label: npm Docs: package.json
sourceReferences.note: Used for package manifest fields, dependencies, scripts, and metadata.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js API: Packages
sourceReferences.note: Used for Node.js package entry points and package type behavior.

intent: Check whether the candidate can distinguish runtime dependencies from development-only dependencies and understand deployment impact.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between dependencies and devDependencies?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that dependencies are packages needed by the application or library at runtime.

requirements.description: Defines dependencies as packages needed when the app or library runs
Connects runtime dependencies to production execution, not only local development
Distinguishes runtime packages from build, lint, test, or local tooling packages

expectedSignals.label: Explains that devDependencies are packages needed for development, testing, linting, formatting, building, or local tooling.

requirements.description: Defines devDependencies as packages used during development or project maintenance
Mentions testing, linting, formatting, building, or local tool use cases
Explains that devDependencies are not normally required by the running production app

expectedSignals.label: Gives practical examples such as express in dependencies and jest, eslint, typescript, or nodemon in devDependencies depending on the project.

requirements.description: Gives a runtime package example such as express in dependencies
Gives development tool examples such as jest, eslint, typescript, or nodemon
Explains why each example belongs to runtime or development based on when it is used

expectedSignals.label: Mentions npm install --save-dev or npm install -D for adding development dependencies.

requirements.description: Mentions npm install --save-dev as a way to add a devDependency
Mentions npm install -D as the shorthand form
Explains that the command records the package under devDependencies in package.json

expectedSignals.label: Understands production installs may omit devDependencies, so putting runtime packages in devDependencies can break production, while putting dev tools in dependencies can bloat deploys.

requirements.description: Mentions that production installs may omit devDependencies
Explains that putting a runtime package in devDependencies can break production startup or imports
Explains that putting dev tools in dependencies can increase production install size or attack surface

scoringHints.description: Clearly distinguishes runtime and development dependency categories, gives examples, and explains production install consequences.
Understands the basic distinction but misses deployment or install-mode implications.
Can define the words but cannot apply them to real packages.
Answer would likely create broken or bloated production deployments.

followUps.question: Where would you put TypeScript in a project that compiles to JavaScript before deployment?
followUps.purpose: Probe build-time versus runtime dependency reasoning.

followUps.question: What can happen if express is placed only in devDependencies and the production install omits dev dependencies?

en.title: dependencies versus devDependencies
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between dependencies and devDependencies?
en.displayIntent: Check runtime versus development dependency understanding.
guidance: Define runtime dependencies.
Define development-only dependencies.
Explain production install impact.
commonMistakes: Putting runtime packages in devDependencies.
Assuming the distinction never matters.
Not knowing development-only examples.

vi.title: dependencies và devDependencies
vi.displayQuestion: dependencies và devDependencies khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết về dependency runtime và dependency chỉ dùng khi phát triển.
guidance: Định nghĩa dependency cần ở runtime.
Định nghĩa dependency chỉ dùng cho development.
Giải thích ảnh hưởng khi install production.
commonMistakes: Đặt package runtime vào devDependencies.
Tưởng phân biệt này không bao giờ quan trọng.
Không nêu được ví dụ dependency chỉ dùng khi dev.

ja.title: dependencies と devDependencies
ja.displayQuestion: dependencies と devDependencies はどう違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: runtime dependency と development-only dependency の理解を確認する。
guidance: runtime dependency を定義する。
development-only dependency を定義する。
production install への影響を説明する。
commonMistakes: runtime package を devDependencies に置く。
この区別は重要ではないと思う。
development-only dependency の例を挙げられない。

sourceReferences.note: Used for dependencies and devDependencies manifest fields.

intent: Verify that the candidate understands package-lock.json as a reproducibility and dependency-tree control mechanism.
primaryQuestion: What is package-lock.json, and why should it be committed?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that package-lock.json records the exact resolved dependency tree, including transitive dependencies.

requirements.description: Explains that package-lock.json records the exact resolved dependency tree
Mentions transitive dependencies, not only direct package.json dependencies
Contrasts the resolved tree with package.json version ranges

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that it captures resolved versions, package sources, and integrity information used by npm.

requirements.description: Mentions exact resolved package versions in the lockfile
Mentions resolved package sources or tarball URLs
Mentions integrity metadata used by npm to verify packages

expectedSignals.label: Explains that committing it helps teammates, CI, and production builds install the same dependency versions.

requirements.description: Explains that committing the lockfile keeps teammate installs consistent
Explains that CI uses the lockfile for repeatable dependency installs
Connects committed lockfiles to more reproducible production builds

expectedSignals.label: Connects the lockfile to reliable npm ci installs and easier review of dependency changes.

requirements.description: Explains that npm ci installs from package-lock.json
Mentions that npm ci expects package.json and the lockfile to agree
Explains that lockfile diffs make dependency changes more visible for review

expectedSignals.label: Understands that package-lock.json is different from node_modules: the lockfile is committed, while installed package files usually are not.

requirements.description: Distinguishes lockfile metadata from installed dependency files in node_modules
States that package-lock.json is normally committed for applications
States that node_modules is normally generated by npm install or npm ci and not committed

scoringHints.description: Explains exact dependency tree, transitive versions, integrity, reproducible installs, npm ci, and why it belongs in source control.
Knows it locks versions and should be committed but misses transitive dependencies or integrity detail.
Has seen lockfiles but cannot explain what problem they solve.
Answer would undermine reproducible builds or dependency review.

followUps.question: What should happen in CI if package.json and package-lock.json disagree?
followUps.purpose: Connect lockfile understanding to npm ci behavior.

followUps.question: Why is committing node_modules not the same as committing package-lock.json?
followUps.purpose: Separate installed artifacts from reproducible dependency metadata.

en.title: package-lock.json purpose
en.displayQuestion: What is package-lock.json, and why should it be committed?
en.displayIntent: Check lockfile and reproducible install understanding.
guidance: Explain exact resolved dependency tree.
Mention transitive dependencies and integrity.
Connect committing the file to CI and teammate consistency.
commonMistakes: Thinking package.json alone is enough for exact installs.
Confusing the lockfile with node_modules.
Deleting lockfile changes without understanding them.

vi.title: Mục đích của package-lock.json
vi.displayQuestion: package-lock.json là gì, và vì sao nên commit nó?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết về lockfile và install có thể tái lập.
guidance: Giải thích cây dependency đã resolve chính xác.
Nhắc tới transitive dependency và integrity.
Liên hệ việc commit file với CI và sự nhất quán giữa các máy.
commonMistakes: Tưởng package.json đủ để install chính xác tuyệt đối.
Nhầm lockfile với node_modules.
Xóa thay đổi lockfile mà không hiểu.

ja.title: package-lock.json の目的
ja.displayQuestion: package-lock.json とは何で、なぜ commit すべきですか。
ja.displayIntent: lockfile と reproducible install の理解を確認する。
guidance: 正確に解決された dependency tree を説明する。
transitive dependency と integrity に触れる。
commit する理由を CI や team consistency と結びつける。
commonMistakes: package.json だけで完全に同じ install になると思う。
lockfile と node_modules を混同する。
lockfile の変更を理解せず削除する。

intent: Check whether the candidate can choose between npm install and npm ci for local development versus repeatable CI builds.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between npm install and npm ci?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that npm install installs dependencies and may update package-lock.json when dependency resolution changes.

requirements.description: Explains that npm install installs dependencies for the project
Mentions that npm install may resolve package.json version ranges
Mentions that dependency resolution changes can update package-lock.json

expectedSignals.label: Explains that npm install is commonly used locally and when adding or changing packages.

requirements.description: Identifies npm install as common in local development
Mentions using npm install when adding a new package
Mentions using npm install when changing dependency declarations

expectedSignals.label: Explains that npm ci performs a clean install based on package-lock.json and removes existing node_modules before installing.

requirements.description: Explains that npm ci installs based on package-lock.json
Describes npm ci as a clean install command
Mentions that npm ci removes existing node_modules before installing

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that npm ci fails when package.json and package-lock.json are out of sync instead of silently rewriting the lockfile.

requirements.description: Mentions that npm ci checks package.json and package-lock.json consistency
Explains that npm ci fails when the manifest and lockfile are out of sync
Explains that npm ci does not silently rewrite the lockfile to resolve mismatches

expectedSignals.label: Identifies npm ci as the preferred command for CI or reproducible build environments.

requirements.description: Identifies npm ci as preferred in CI pipelines
Connects npm ci to reproducible dependency installs
Explains that npm ci enforces lockfile discipline in build environments

scoringHints.description: Contrasts local install and clean CI install, including lockfile requirements, mismatch failure, node_modules removal, and reproducibility.
Knows npm ci is for CI and uses the lockfile but misses one behavior such as removal or mismatch failure.
Has used both commands but cannot explain operational differences.
Answer would allow nondeterministic or broken CI dependency installs.

followUps.question: Why is npm ci usually safer than npm install in a CI pipeline?

en.title: npm install versus npm ci
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between npm install and npm ci?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands repeatable dependency installation.
guidance: Explain what npm install can change.
Explain npm ci clean install behavior.
Connect npm ci to CI reproducibility.
commonMistakes: Calling npm ci only a faster install.
Forgetting lockfile mismatch failures.
Not knowing node_modules is removed.

vi.title: npm install và npm ci
vi.displayQuestion: npm install và npm ci khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết về install dependency có thể tái lập.
guidance: Giải thích npm install có thể thay đổi gì.
Giải thích behavior clean install của npm ci.
Liên hệ npm ci với tính ổn định trong CI.

Quên trường hợp package.json và lockfile lệch nhau.
Không biết node_modules bị xóa trước khi npm ci install.

ja.title: npm install と npm ci
ja.displayQuestion: npm install と npm ci はどう違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: 再現可能な dependency installation を理解しているか確認する。
guidance: npm install が何を変更し得るか説明する。
npm ci の clean install behavior を説明する。
npm ci を CI reproducibility と結びつける。

lockfile mismatch failure を忘れる。

intent: Check whether the candidate understands node_modules as an installed dependency artifact and why teams usually avoid committing it.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that Node.js module resolution commonly looks in node_modules when resolving package imports.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that node_modules is usually large, generated, and can contain many platform-specific or environment-specific files.

requirements.description: Mentions that node_modules can be very large
Explains that node_modules is generated by package installation

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that committing node_modules causes noisy diffs, repository bloat, and stale dependency artifacts in most application workflows.

intent: Check whether the candidate can explain semantic versioning and common npm range operators well enough to avoid accidental dependency upgrades.
primaryQuestion: What is semantic versioning, and what do ^ and ~ mean?

expectedSignals.label: Explains semantic versioning as MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH.

requirements.description: Names the MAJOR, MINOR, and PATCH positions
Maps the positions to a version example such as 1.2.3
Identifies this format as semantic versioning

expectedSignals.label: States that major versions indicate breaking changes, minor versions add backward-compatible functionality, and patch versions fix backward-compatible bugs.

requirements.description: States that major version changes indicate breaking changes
States that minor version changes add backward-compatible functionality
States that patch version changes are backward-compatible bug fixes

expectedSignals.label: Explains that caret ranges such as ^1.2.3 generally allow compatible updates below the next major version, such as 1.x.x but not 2.0.0.

requirements.description: Explains the caret range behavior for a normal 1.x version
States that ^1.2.3 can allow later 1.x.x compatible releases
States that ^1.2.3 does not allow 2.0.0

expectedSignals.label: Explains that tilde ranges such as ~1.2.3 generally allow patch updates within the same minor version, such as 1.2.x but not 1.3.0.

requirements.description: Explains the tilde range behavior for a normal 1.2.x version
States that ~1.2.3 can allow later 1.2.x patch releases
States that ~1.2.3 does not allow 1.3.0

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that lockfiles pin the exact installed version even when package.json contains a range, and dependency updates should still be reviewed and tested.

requirements.description: Explains that the lockfile records the exact installed version
Distinguishes package.json ranges from the version actually installed by npm ci
Mentions that dependency updates should still be reviewed and tested despite semver

scoringHints.description: Explains MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH, ^ and ~ ranges, lockfile interaction, and the need to test dependency updates.
Correctly explains semver and basic ranges but misses lockfile or real-world trust limitations.
Has a rough idea of version numbers but cannot reason about allowed upgrades.
Answer would lead to unsafe or misunderstood dependency upgrades.

en.title: Semantic versioning ranges

Explain ^ and ~ with examples.
Mention lockfiles and testing dependency updates.
commonMistakes: Treating ^ and ~ as identical.
Assuming semver prevents all breakages.
Ignoring the exact locked version.

Bỏ qua version chính xác trong lockfile.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands local versus global npm installs and why project tooling should usually be local.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that a global package is installed into npm's global prefix and is usually used for command-line tools available across projects.

requirements.description: Explains that global packages are installed in npm's global prefix

Explains that global tools are available across projects on that machine

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that application code normally resolves imports from local project dependencies, not arbitrary global packages.

requirements.description: States that application imports normally resolve from local project dependencies
Explains that a global install is not a substitute for declaring a runtime dependency
Explains that relying on global packages creates hidden environment requirements

expectedSignals.label: Explains that local dev tools can be run through npm scripts or npx, helping every teammate and CI use the project-defined version.

requirements.description: Mentions running local dev tools through npm scripts

intent: Check whether the candidate can describe how to create and export a simple local module using either CommonJS or ES Modules.

expectedSignals.label: Shows a CommonJS approach such as assigning module.exports or properties on exports, then importing with require and a relative path.

expectedSignals.label: Shows an ES Module approach such as export function, export const, or export default, then importing with import syntax.

requirements.description: Shows or describes a named export such as export function or export const
Mentions export default when describing ES Module options
Shows or describes importing the exported binding with import syntax

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that the project module system must match Node's configuration, such as package.json type or .cjs/.mjs extensions.

requirements.description: Explains that import/export syntax must match the project's module mode

Mentions .cjs or .mjs extensions as module mode signals

expectedSignals.label: Understands that module code should avoid unnecessary side effects at import time, especially for reusable helpers.

intent: Check whether the candidate can distinguish callback, synchronous, and promise-based filesystem APIs and understand their event loop impact.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between fs.readFile, fs.readFileSync, and fs/promises?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that fs.readFile is asynchronous and uses a callback with an error-first pattern when the read completes.

requirements.description: Explains that fs.readFile is asynchronous and callback-based
Mentions the error-first callback pattern
Explains that the callback runs when the file read completes or fails

expectedSignals.label: Explains that fs.readFileSync blocks the current JavaScript thread until the file read finishes or throws an error.

requirements.description: Explains that fs.readFileSync blocks the JavaScript thread
Mentions that execution waits until the file read finishes
Mentions that readFileSync throws errors instead of using callback or promise rejection

expectedSignals.label: Explains that fs/promises provides promise-based filesystem APIs that work naturally with async/await and reject on errors.

requirements.description: Explains that fs/promises exposes promise-based filesystem APIs
Mentions using fs/promises naturally with async/await
Mentions that errors are handled as promise rejections

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that all three read the whole file into memory, so streams may be better for large files.

requirements.description: Mentions that readFile-style APIs read the whole file into memory
Explains why large files can create memory pressure
Suggests streams or incremental processing for large files

expectedSignals.label: Connects synchronous filesystem calls to event loop blocking, especially inside request handlers, while asynchronous forms let the server continue handling other work.

requirements.description: Connects synchronous filesystem calls to event loop blocking
Explains why blocking is risky inside API request handlers
Explains that asynchronous forms allow the server to continue handling other work while I/O is pending

scoringHints.description: Contrasts callback, sync, and promise APIs; covers error handling, event loop blocking, async/await usage, and whole-file memory behavior.
Knows sync versus async and promises but misses memory or error-handling nuance.
Can name the APIs but cannot choose safely in request-serving code.
Answer would likely block the event loop or mishandle filesystem errors.

ja.displayIntent: Node.js の binary data と encoding の基礎を確認する。
guidance: Buffer を raw bytes として定義する。
filesystem、streams、uploads、crypto、base64 の例を挙げる。
encoding conversion と byte length の問題を説明する。
commonMistakes: Buffer を文字配列として扱う。
encoding を無視する。
base64 と encryption を混同する。

sourceReferences.label: Node.js API: Buffer
sourceReferences.note: Used for Buffer, encodings, byte length, and allocation behavior.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js API: File system
sourceReferences.note: Used for filesystem APIs that may return Buffer objects.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands why synchronous filesystem work in request handlers can harm Node.js server responsiveness.
primaryQuestion: Why should you avoid synchronous filesystem calls inside API request handlers?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that synchronous filesystem calls block the Node.js event loop while the operation runs.

requirements.description: Explains that synchronous filesystem calls block the event loop
Mentions that JavaScript cannot continue while the synchronous operation runs
Connects filesystem blocking to request-serving code, not only scripts

expectedSignals.label: Connects event loop blocking to worse latency and throughput because other requests cannot be processed by the JavaScript thread during that time.

requirements.description: Explains that blocking increases response latency
Explains that blocking reduces throughput under load
Explains that other requests cannot be processed by the JavaScript thread during the block

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that a single slow disk operation or large file read can affect unrelated users, not just the current request.

requirements.description: Explains that one slow filesystem operation can delay multiple requests
Mentions large file reads as a concrete blocking risk
States that unrelated users or requests can be affected

expectedSignals.label: Suggests alternatives such as fs/promises, callback-based fs APIs, streaming, caching data at startup, moving work outside the request path, or offloading heavy work.

requirements.description: Suggests fs/promises or callback-based asynchronous filesystem APIs
Suggests streaming large files or caching data at startup when appropriate
Suggests moving or offloading heavy work outside the request path

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes exceptions and nuance: small synchronous reads during startup may be acceptable, but per-request synchronous I/O in a server is risky.

requirements.description: Recognizes that small synchronous reads during startup can be acceptable
States that per-request synchronous I/O in a server is risky
Distinguishes one-time startup work from repeated request-path work

scoringHints.description: Explains event loop blocking, latency and throughput impact, cross-request effects, safer alternatives, and startup-versus-request nuance.
Knows sync calls block Node.js and suggests async alternatives but misses broader concurrency or parsing concerns.
Repeats that async is better but cannot explain the server impact.
Answer would allow blocking request handlers in production APIs.

followUps.question: If every request calls fs.readFileSync on a large JSON file, what happens under concurrent traffic?
When might a synchronous filesystem call be acceptable in a Node.js service?
followUps.purpose: Check practical nuance instead of rote avoidance.

en.title: Synchronous filesystem calls in APIs
en.displayQuestion: Why should you avoid synchronous filesystem calls inside API request handlers?
en.displayIntent: Check event loop and request-concurrency impact understanding.
guidance: Explain event loop blocking.
Connect blocking to latency and throughput.
Suggest async, streaming, caching, or moving work out of the request path.
Mention startup-time exceptions.
commonMistakes: Thinking sync I/O only affects one request.
Ignoring CPU parsing after async reads.
Treating all sync calls as equally acceptable in servers.

vi.title: Filesystem sync trong API
vi.displayQuestion: Vì sao nên tránh synchronous filesystem calls bên trong API request handler?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết về event loop và ảnh hưởng tới concurrent request.
guidance: Giải thích event loop bị block.
Liên hệ blocking với latency và throughput.
Đề xuất async, streaming, cache hoặc chuyển việc ra khỏi request path.
Nhắc ngoại lệ khi chạy lúc startup.
commonMistakes: Tưởng sync I/O chỉ ảnh hưởng một request.
Bỏ qua CPU parsing sau khi đọc async.
Xem mọi sync call đều ổn trong server.

ja.title: API 内の synchronous filesystem calls
ja.displayQuestion: API request handler の中で synchronous filesystem calls を避けるべきなのはなぜですか。
ja.displayIntent: event loop と request concurrency への影響を理解しているか確認する。
guidance: event loop blocking を説明する。
blocking を latency と throughput に結びつける。
async、streaming、caching、request path 外への移動を提案する。
startup-time exception に触れる。
commonMistakes: sync I/O は 1 request だけに影響すると思う。
async read 後の CPU parsing を無視する。
server での sync call をすべて問題ないと考える。

sourceReferences.label: Node.js Learn: Don't Block the Event Loop
sourceReferences.note: Used for event loop blocking, worker pool, and server responsiveness guidance.

sourceReferences.label: Node.js API: File system
sourceReferences.note: Used for synchronous and asynchronous filesystem API behavior.
